glossary.definition: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) .

glossary.definition: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.sentence: Regression is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc. Therefore, for a new data object, the machine learning algorithm finds a prediction, for example, the prediction of the possible price of a house, the prediction of the potential number of graduating students, etc.

glossary.definition: It is a type of learning based on unlabelled data. It can be characterised as 'I am self-sufficient in learning!' according to (Atul, 2019). The goal is to reveal patterns/structure of data and it provides descriptive models.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning is also called learning without a teacher. This type of learning is based on unlabelled data (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). We can compare it with a child that has a set of bricks of different colours in front of him. Looking at them, he/she starts dividing them into different groups based on their colours. According to (Atul, 2019), unsupervised learning can be characterised as "I am self-sufficient in learning!". The goal of this learning is to reveal patterns/structure of data. Unsupervised learning provides descriptive models.

glossary.definition: a process of dividing a dataset into subsets (clusters) where data objects within each subset share similarities, commonly used in unsupervised machine learning.
glossary.sentence: One of the tasks of unsupervised machine learning is clustering . It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: It is a reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as "My life-my rules!". It is suitable for agents who operate in a changing environment and need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as "My life-my rules!". Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game-playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: an algorithm that determines the output value for a new data object by examining the k nearest neighbours in the dataset, based on calculated distances and selecting the mode or mean of their labels.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset; store the index of the data objects and the calculated distances in a list; sort the list of data objects and distances in ascending order from the smallest distance to the largest one; pick up the top k data objects from the sorted list; get the labels of the selected data objects; identify the output for the new data object: in the classification task, the algorithm will return the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class); in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: a version of the kNN algorithm where only one nearest neighbour is considered for classification.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: a version of the kNN algorithm where a hyperparameter k is used to determine the number of nearest neighbours considered for classification.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: a method used by the algorithm to determine the class label for a new data object based on the majority vote of its nearest neighbours.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).

glossary.definition: a voting method where the class label that receives the most votes from the nearest neighbours determines the label for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).

glossary.definition: a representation of data objects in terms of their feature values, used as input for clustering algorithms like K-Means.
glossary.sentence: data objects represented by feature vectors)

glossary.definition: labels assigned to data objects indicating their category or class in a classification problem.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm should identify class labels for two new data objects represented in yellow.

glossary.sentence: you need to define the value of the hyperparameter k , which represents the chosen number of neighbours that will be examined.

glossary.definition: a supervised machine learning task where the goal is to predict continuous values
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range.

glossary.definition: the average position of all data objects in a cluster, around which the K-Means algorithm groups data objects based on distance to the centroid.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that partitions data into K clusters by iteratively assigning data objects to centroids and recalculating centroid positions until convergence.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), "the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement" .

glossary.definition: A group of data objects that are similar to each other based on certain features or characteristics.
glossary.sentence: The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster.

glossary.definition: a measure of how alike two data objects are, determined by the distance between them; used in kNN and unsupervised clustering algorithms.
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm. It is also used in unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: a process where each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster until one cluster with all data objects is acquired.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster. The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) . Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all dataity objects is acquired at the highest level.

glossary.definition: a top-down clustering approach that starts with a cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits existing clusters into two new clusters until each data object forms its own cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters. The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) . Finally, the clustering stops when each data object makes a cluster.

glossary.definition: a measure used in clustering algorithms to determine how close data objects are to each other or to a centroid, such as Euclidean or Manhattan distance.
glossary.sentence: A distance represents a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters (see above).

glossary.definition: the specific numerical values of the centroid position, which are updated during the K-Means algorithm's iteration process.
glossary.sentence: Repeat Steps 3-4 until the values of centroids do not change. The mentioned steps are represented in Figure 1. The typical termination criteria of the clustering process in the K-Means algorithm is an iteration when data objects do not change their cluster membership (centroid values do not change) (Jones, 2009; Kubat, 2017; Tyugu , 2007).

glossary.definition: a method of selecting the number of clusters in K-Means by experimenting with different values until an optimal result is achieved.
glossary.sentence: Usually, it is selected by a trial-and-error approach.

glossary.definition: datasets used in unsupervised learning that do not have predefined class labels, allowing for the discovery of underlying patterns.
glossary.sentence: unsupervised learning uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.

glossary.definition: the process of examining data to understand its structure and characteristics, often performed before clustering algorithms are applied.
glossary.sentence: In many cases, clustering is used in the data exploration step (see Topic 4.1) to understand the structure of data.

glossary.definition: the process of choosing values for hyperparameters like k in the kNN algorithm before training begins.
glossary.sentence: before you start using the algorithm, you need to define the value of the hyperparameter k , which represents the chosen number of neighbours that will be examined.

glossary.definition: the process of selecting initial centroid positions in the K-Means algorithm, which can affect the clustering results.
glossary.sentence: the initialisation of centroids can also be problematic.

glossary.definition: methods used to measure the similarity between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods . The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H. Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H. Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: an output of hierarchical clustering that is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017) , “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format” . Thus, it provides insight into the way how clusters were formed.

glossary.definition: the degree of difference between two groups when they are being merged in hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: the degree of difference between two groups when they are being split in divisive hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: a node in a dendrogram that represents the merging of data objects into clusters.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: the value indicating the similarity between two data objects at a specific level of hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The key to interpreting a dendrogram is to focus on the height of clades, that is, the height at which two data objects are merged. The height of clades serves as an indicator of cluster similarity: Two data objects (leaves) in the same clade are more similar than two data objects (leaves) in another clade.

glossary.definition: a horizontal line drawn on a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm. The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: a horizontal line used in dendrogram analysis to determine the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm. The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: a line representing a cluster in a dendrogram, intersected by a horizontal cut-off line to determine the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters. Cut-offs can be performed at different levels of the hierarchy leading to a different number of clusters.

glossary.definition: a method used in divisive hierarchical clustering to perform recursive clustering on intermediate clusters until all data objects are included or the minimum number is reached.
glossary.sentence: However, Pai (2021) indicates that one of the methods to implement this type of hierarchical clustering is to perform the procedure of K-Means recursively on each intermediate cluster till you encounter all the data objects in the dataset or the minimum number of data objects you desire to have in a cluster.

glossary.definition: an ordered sequence of cluster merges representing the entire structure of hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Summarising the information about the hierarchical clustering given in this topic, it is worth mentioning that the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges (Hastie, 2017) .

glossary.definition: parameters of a machine learning algorithm that are set by the developer to control the behavior of the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Metrics depend on a task, whether it is classification, regression, or clustering. The metrics are used to compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: a measure used to assess the performance of a machine learning model in comparison to other models or different configurations of the same model.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: the output of a model in a regression task that are generated by the algorithm
glossary.sentence: actual labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted labels.

glossary.definition: a metric that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: units of measurement that are the square of the quantity being estimated
glossary.sentence: This metric has the same units of measurement as the quantity being estimated. Still, they are squared units;

glossary.definition: data points that deviate significantly from other observations in a dataset
glossary.sentence: This type of error is prone to outliers.

glossary.definition: a metric that applies a square root to MSE, providing an error measure in the same unit as the target variable
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable

glossary.definition: a metric that calculates the average of the absolute differences between predicted and actual output values
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: a metric that indicates how close the model fits the actual data objects in a dataset, with values ranging from 0 to 1
glossary.sentence: The R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.

glossary.definition: the correct labels present in the dataset that are used to assess the correctness of predicted class labels
glossary.sentence: Since the classification is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual class labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted class labels.

glossary.definition: a method in multiclass classification where each class is treated as a positive class and others as negative
glossary.sentence: the typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: a data object that is correctly predicted as being in the positive class
glossary.sentence: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: a data object that is correctly predicted as being in the negative class
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: a data object that is incorrectly predicted as being in the positive class when it is actually negative
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not.

glossary.definition: a data object that is incorrectly predicted as being in the negative class when it is actually positive
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam.

glossary.definition: a two-dimensional table that summarizes the number of correct and incorrect predictions made by a classifier
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017). It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category.

glossary.definition: a metric that evaluates the fraction of correct predictions made by a classifier over a given set of data objects
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: a situation in which the distribution of class labels in a dataset is not even, leading to potential bias in model performance evaluation
glossary.sentence: However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A measure used in clustering analysis to evaluate how similar a data object is to its own cluster compared to other clusters.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A graphical representation of Silhouette Scores, with the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A designation assigned to a group of data objects within a clustering algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A metric that quantifies how close a data object is to its own cluster compared to other clusters, with values ranging from -1 to 1.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning where each training example is associated with a correct output label, allowing the model to learn by comparing its predictions to the known correct outputs
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning where the model is trained on unlabeled data, with the goal of finding hidden patterns or structures in the data.
glossary.sentence: artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.

glossary.definition: A subset of machine learning that involves artificial neural networks with multiple layers, capable of learning complex patterns in data.
glossary.sentence: Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: A specialized cell in the human brain that receives and transmits signals, forming the basis of all mental activity.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: A branched extension of a neuron that receives signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A long, slender projection of a neuron that transmits electrical signals to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.

glossary.definition: A junction between two neurons where signals are transmitted from one neuron to another.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: a processing unit in an artificial neural network that applies the summation and activation functions to produce an output signal
glossary.sentence: Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.

glossary.definition: The combined effect of electrical and chemical processes in neurons that underlie brain function and mental activities.
glossary.sentence: the human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A measure of the strength of a connection between two neurons, determined by the amount of electrical current it must carry.
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight).

glossary.definition: A type of synapse that enhances the activity of a neuron, typically associated with positive synaptic weights.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A type of synapse that reduces the activity of a neuron, typically associated with negative synaptic weights.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A computational system composed of interconnected artificial neurons that processes information through layers of processing units.
glossary.sentence: artificial neural networks are experiencing their heyday. Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Computational systems that are modeled after the structure and function of biological neurons, particularly the human brain.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.”

glossary.definition: A computational unit in artificial neural networks that mimics the function of a biological neuron, receiving and processing inputs to produce an output.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Signals received by an artificial neuron from the environment or other neurons, represented as numerical values.
glossary.sentence: one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons.

glossary.definition: Numerical values assigned to inputs in an artificial neuron that indicate their importance in determining the output of the network.
glossary.sentence: a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output.

glossary.definition: The result produced by an artificial neuron, which can be a final solution of the network or an input to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: a function used to transform the weighted sum of inputs into an output value
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: an additional input added to each neuron in every layer
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input.

glossary.definition: a function that combines input signals into a single value, which is then used as the input to the activation function
glossary.sentence: Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.

glossary.definition: a function that determines the output of a neuron based on its input signal
glossary.sentence: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron.

glossary.definition: an activation function that outputs a binary value, typically 0 or 1, based on whether the input signal meets a certain threshold
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

glossary.definition: an activation function that outputs a value of either +1 or -1 depending on the sign of the input signal
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

glossary.definition: an activation function that outputs a value proportional to its input signal, effectively passing the input unchanged through the function
glossary.sentence: the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: an activation function that outputs a value between 0 and 1, typically used for binary classification tasks
glossary.sentence: the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: a computational model inspired by the structure and function of biological neurons, consisting of interconnected processing units (neurons) that process information through weighted connections
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural network An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern.

glossary.definition: a neuron in a neural network that performs computations based on weighted inputs and an activation function
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: a pathway through which signals are transmitted between processing units in an artificial neural network
glossary.sentence: They are connected by transmitting signals to each other.

glossary.definition: the first layer of a neural network that receives input data, with each node representing a feature or variable in the input
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: the final layer of a neural network that produces the output or prediction for a given input, with each node corresponding to a specific output value
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: a layer in a neural network that is neither the input nor the output layer, responsible for processing and transforming input data into higher-level features or representations
glossary.sentence: from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: a method of processing information simultaneously across multiple neurons in a neural network, allowing for faster computation
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks have a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently.

glossary.definition: a type of neural network where connections between neurons are directed in one direction, with no loops or cycles
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one direction. Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: a type of neural network where connections allow signals to flow in cycles, enabling the network to maintain a state over time
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: a method used to adjust the weights in a neural network based on the difference between the actual output and the desired output
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: a parameter in a neural network that determines the strength of the connection between two neurons, with values typically initialized randomly and adjusted during training
glossary.sentence: assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set;

glossary.definition: the ability of a neural network to continue functioning effectively even if some processing units or connections are damaged
glossary.sentence: artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation.

glossary.definition: a term used to describe the ability of a neural network to maintain functionality even when some components are damaged or not working properly
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network.

glossary.definition: the task of forecasting future outcomes based on current or historical data
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects;

glossary.definition: the task of finding the best solution or arrangement that satisfies certain constraints
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints;

glossary.definition: the task of separating signal from background noise to extract relevant information
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: the task of determining values that depend on other variables in a complex way
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: a neural network with only one processing layer; all inputs are connected directly to the outputs
glossary.sentence: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.

glossary.definition: a specific instance of input data used to train a neural network, which includes both the input features and the corresponding correct output label
glossary.sentence: selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network;

glossary.definition: a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1
glossary.sentence: A learning rate is one of the hyperparameters used in artificial neural networks. It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1.

glossary.definition: the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset
glossary.sentence: Another hyperparameter is the number of epochs . An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes. Therefore, the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: a type of artificial neural network that consists of multiple layers of nodes in a directed graph, with each layer consisting of an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer; the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved (see above).

glossary.definition: a neural network that has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity
glossary.sentence: a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: the process of adding all the inputs together before applying the activation function
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: a method used to compute the weight correction in the learning process of a perceptron
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule : Therefore, the complete rule for adjustments of the weights of the perceptron is the following one:

glossary.definition: the difference between the actual output and the desired output in a training example
glossary.sentence: The learning can be stopped if the total classification error on all training examples reaches the pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved.

glossary.definition: the structure of a neural network, including the number and arrangement of layers and neurons
glossary.sentence: The architecture of such a neural network is given in Figure 7.

glossary.definition: a type of neural network where information flows in one direction, from input to output layers
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a constant value added to the input of a neuron to shift the activation function's threshold
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input.

glossary.definition: a method used in training artificial neural networks where the error from the output layer is propagated backward through the network to adjust the weights of the connections between neurons, allowing the network to learn from its mistakes
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: the process of passing input data through a neural network from the input layer to the output layer without any backward propagation of errors
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding . In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: the difference between the predicted output of a neural network and the actual desired output, which is used to adjust the weights during training
glossary.sentence: However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output. Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: an intelligent computer system that uses knowledge to solve problems or make decisions
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a collection of facts, rules, and other information used by an expert system to make decisions
glossary.sentence: The structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: the component of an expert system that applies rules to facts and determines the logical consequences of those facts
glossary.sentence: The inference engine is the mechanism for processing knowledge in an expert system.

glossary.definition: the process of finding a solution to a problem through reasoning, analysis, and decision-making
glossary.sentence: However, the problem-solving process is possible not only because a person is able to determine its initial state, goal state, and alternative courses of available actions, but also because humans use general knowledge of a problem domain when dealing with problems.

glossary.definition: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: a structure for representing knowledge, such as semantic networks or frames
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.definition: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge.

glossary.definition: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert.

glossary.definition: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: a computer system designed to solve complex problems by mimicking human expertise
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”.

glossary.definition: There are several main methods used for this purpose (Hopgood, 2001): 1) knowledge is acquired from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base, 2) the domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, and 3) nowadays intelligent computer systems are able to learn from examples.
glossary.sentence: How does knowledge get to the knowledge base? There are several main methods used for this purpose (Hopgood, 2001): 1) knowledge is acquired from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base, 2) the domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, and 3) nowadays intelligent computer systems are able to learn from examples.

glossary.definition: knowledge stored at the level of a concept it belongs to
glossary.sentence: The researchers also have revealed that exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.

glossary.definition: time taken to respond to a question
glossary.sentence: the mean reaction time to the question “Can an ostrich fly?” was smaller than to the question “Can an ostrich breathe?”

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: domain object/concept that is represented as a labelled node in the graph
glossary.sentence: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.

glossary.definition: a connection between concepts represented as directed labelled arcs in the graph
glossary.sentence: directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of "is a" (also "is a subclass of", "is a subset of", "is a type of" or "is a kind of")
glossary.sentence: A relation "subclass-class" (also "subset-set", "subtype-type", " subkind -kind") is represented by a title of "is a" (also "is a subclass of", "is a subset of", "is a type of" or "is a kind of").

glossary.definition: a set of concepts/objects that share common properties
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of "is an instance/example of"
glossary.sentence: A relation "instance-class/set/type/kind" is represented by the title of "is an instance/example of".

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of "Part of"
glossary.sentence: A relation "part-whole" is represented by the title of "Part of" (Fig.8).

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of "property/has a property of"
glossary.sentence: A relation "object-property" is represented by the title of "property/has a property of".

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of "value"
glossary.sentence: A relation "property-value" is represented by the title of "value". This relation can come only after the relation "property/has a property of".

glossary.definition: any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: the process by which a subclass inherits properties and slots from its parent class
glossary.sentence: The inference engine searches for the specified property of the object until its value is obtained ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a structured unit in a knowledge base that contains slots for storing information about an object or concept
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: A system that captures and represents knowledge using frames.
glossary.sentence: Frames are used to capture and represent knowledge in a frame-based expert system.

glossary.definition: A property of objects/concepts. Each slot has a value that can be symbolic, numerical, boolean, empty, or contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Each frame has a unique title or identifier in the knowledge base. Slots are properties of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames that represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: a specific value assigned to a slot in a frame
glossary.sentence: Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: A relationship that relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame, allowing hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is at the highest level and the most specific ones at lower levels.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame.

glossary.definition: A relationship that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts by associating frames corresponding to components with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: The aggregation relation allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.

glossary.definition: A relationship that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation. It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A feature where a slot can have a procedural attachment, allowing the addition of regular procedures and/or demons for tasks like generating images, calculating values, or acquiring data.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment. In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system for purposes like generating images, calculating numerical values, or acquiring data from external sources.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system. We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A type of frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. It helps represent a problem in an abstract form.
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form.

glossary.definition: a specific instance of a class or subclass frame, representing an individual object
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: A type of frame that is derived from a class frame, containing new slots that characterize objects belonging to the sub-class. It allows creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5). The sub-class frames can contain new slots that are not included in the class frames but characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.

glossary.definition: A feature where a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously, provided that the slot titles of parent frames are unique.
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: A principle used to relate two frames by using the unique title of one frame as a slot value in another frame.
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A collection of related frames linked together into a system. According to Minsky (1975), 'collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems'.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), 'collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems'.

glossary.definition: actions that modify or interact with the values of slots in a frame
glossary.sentence: Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: frames that define the structure of a class and its subclasses in a knowledge base
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: a framework for organizing knowledge where objects (frames) have properties and methods
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: a demon triggered when a slot value is unspecified and it is necessary to calculate or set it
glossary.sentence: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: a demon triggered when a slot value changes
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: a demon triggered when a slot value is unspecified and it is added to the frame
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: a demon triggered when a slot value is deleted
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: a system that stores and organizes knowledge using frames as units of representation
glossary.sentence: Figure 13 represents a frame-based knowledge base that stores knowledge about persons and their home animals like cats and dogs.

glossary.definition: a slot in a class or subclass frame that defines relationships between frames
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal . It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: connections between different frames that represent relationships such as ownership or fear
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner;

glossary.definition: a framework for organizing knowledge using frames, including their slots and demons
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge;

glossary.definition: a rule that specifies conditions and consequences for problem-solving in an expert system
glossary.sentence: The simplest type of rules is a production rule . Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a rule-based expert system that consists of a knowledge base, working memory, and inference engine
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: an intelligent system where domain knowledge is represented as a set of rules for problem-solving
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a temporary storage area within an expert system where facts are kept for use by inference engines during problem-solving
glossary.sentence: the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: a method of representing knowledge in a computer system through rules and procedures
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems.

glossary.definition: a statement that defines a condition and its consequence for problem-solving in an expert system
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: knowledge that is expressed as procedures or rules for performing tasks
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems.

glossary.definition: A component of a rule-based expert system that stores the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration.
glossary.sentence: The working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A component of a rule-based expert system that is essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result and should explain its reasoning and justify the output.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A component of a rule-based expert system that allows the user to interact with the system through a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface.
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Additional components of a rule-based expert system that can be added to work with external data files and program results.
glossary.sentence: However, additional components, such as external applications and developer interface, can be added as well (Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Additional components of a rule-based expert system that is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base and check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A mechanism in a rule-based expert system that implements a pattern-directed search by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle "Recognize-act" makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: a set of instructions that define how to derive new information from existing knowledge in an expert system
glossary.sentence: It is well known that problem domain experts use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order.

glossary.definition: a collection of all rules that are currently applicable and could be fired by the inference engine
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: the process of selecting which rule to apply when multiple rules are applicable at the same time
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: a method for selecting which rule to apply when multiple rules are applicable at the same time
glossary.sentence: Both the structure and the sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: Three types of strategies used in rule-based expert systems for controlling the search process: selection of search direction, selection of rule sequence and structure, and selection of conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: Data that are assumed to be correct when they are used in a rule-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Measures that indicate the confidence level of conclusions derived in the reasoning process within a rule-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: The working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: an inference engine that starts with known facts and applies rules to derive new facts until the goal is reached
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: an inference engine that starts with a hypothesis and works backward to find supporting facts
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: a strategy where rules are prioritized by an explicitly stated priority value, and the highest-priority rule is selected when multiple rules are applicable
glossary.sentence: to fire rules based on their priorities. Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen.

glossary.definition: a conflict resolution method where a specific rule that processes more information is preferred over a general one
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.

glossary.definition: a strategy where the rule whose antecedent uses data most recently added to working memory is selected first
glossary.sentence: more specific rule fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory.

glossary.definition: the organization of rules within a knowledge base that affects how search is conducted and the efficiency of problem-solving
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: expert systems that use production rules as their primary mechanism for reasoning and problem-solving
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”.

glossary.definition: the characteristic of production rules that allows them to be developed and modified independently without affecting other parts of the system
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions.

glossary.definition: the ability to track the execution of production rules and understand how each step contributes to problem-solving
glossary.sentence: Tracing and explanation. The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules.

glossary.definition: the ability of an expert system to provide a meaningful description of its current state and actions based on the rules applied
glossary.sentence: In addition, because each rule corresponds to a single “chunk” of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action.

glossary.definition: knowledge that lacks sufficient information to fully determine a conclusion or solution
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: knowledge that is not certain and may have varying degrees of reliability or truth
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: a measure used in rule-based systems to indicate the degree of certainty associated with a particular rule or fact
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: the process of changing or updating rules within a system to improve its performance or adapt to new information
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.

glossary.definition: the process of removing specific rules from a system to eliminate redundant or incorrect information
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.

glossary.definition: the process of adding new rules to a system to expand its capabilities or address new problem domains
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.

glossary.definition: the process of gradually adding, deleting, or modifying knowledge (rules) in an expert system to improve its functionality over time
glossary.sentence: This syntactic independence supports the incremental development of expert systems by successively adding, deleting, or modifying the knowledge (rules) of the system.

glossary.definition: the process of altering the collection of facts and rules in an expert system to improve its accuracy or adaptability
glossary.sentence: The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules.

glossary.definition: the ongoing process of ensuring the accuracy, relevance, and up-to-date nature of knowledge stored in an expert system
glossary.sentence: The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules.

glossary.definition: the process of activating and executing a rule within an expert system to derive new information or conclusions
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: the activation of a rule within an expert system to perform its specified action or derive new facts
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
